critic_raw:
This CNN lung cancer detection design is generally well-specified, but contains a baseline comparison problem and an architecture selection issue that undermine the core claim.

The baseline is 'sensitivity achieved by radiologist consensus on the same test set.' The CNN's decision threshold is selected on the validation set to 'match or exceed the radiologist consensus sensitivity.' This introduces a threshold selection bias: the CNN threshold is specifically tuned to be at least as sensitive as the radiologist, which guarantees the CNN cannot appear less sensitive than the radiologist in the primary metric by construction. A fair comparison would report CNN sensitivity at the validation-optimized threshold alongside radiologist sensitivity, without engineering the comparison to preclude the CNN from appearing inferior.

Multiple CNN architectures are evaluated on the validation set (ResNet-50 with ImageNet pretraining, custom 3D volumetric CNN, Vision Transformer) and the best performer is selected. This is valid architecture search, but ImageNet pretraining assumes that low-level features learned on natural color images transfer to CT scan volumetric data. CT data is grayscale with physics-constrained intensity values (Hounsfield units), spatially volumetric, and has acquisition-specific noise characteristics (slice thickness, reconstruction kernel) fundamentally different from ImageNet images. ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet may be a suboptimal starting point, and selecting it based on validation performance risks selecting the architecture that best fits validation idiosyncrasies (scanner-specific patterns) rather than the one with the best inductive bias for the task.

The gold standard label requires histopathology confirmation for positive cases and 24-month follow-up for negative cases. In a 10,000-image archive, not all negative-labeled cases will have completed 24-month follow-up at the time of study (particularly for images near the study end date). Cases with incomplete follow-up are either excluded (reducing sample size) or included with potentially incorrect negative labels (interval cancers labeled as negatives). The design does not address how the archive's temporal extent relative to follow-up requirements determines which records are included.

Stratified splitting preserves institution representation, but does not preserve scanner model or acquisition protocol representation. If CT scanner models differ in slice thickness, reconstruction kernel, or dose level across institutions, and these protocol differences correlate with institution (which is common), then institutional stratification proxies but does not guarantee protocol stratification. A scanner that uses a different reconstruction kernel will produce different texture artifacts that the CNN may detect as cancer-relevant features.
all_issues_raised: The CNN threshold is selected on validation to 'match or exceed' radiologist consensus sensitivity, which guarantees by construction that the CNN cannot appear less sensitive than the radiologist in the primary metric; this engineering of the threshold precludes a fair comparison and introduces threshold selection bias favorable to the CNN.

Architecture selection among ResNet-50 (ImageNet pretrained), custom 3D CNN, and Vision Transformer on the validation set risks selecting the architecture that best fits validation-specific scanner artifacts rather than the one with the best inductive bias for CT-based detection; ImageNet pretraining is a particularly weak starting point for grayscale volumetric medical data.

Cases near the study end date will not have completed the 24-month follow-up required for negative label confirmation; these cases are either excluded (reducing sample size and potentially biasing toward older, more complete records) or included with potentially incorrect negative labels, neither of which is addressed in the design.

Institutional stratification does not guarantee CT acquisition protocol stratification (scanner model, slice thickness, reconstruction kernel); protocol differences that correlate with institution will create uncontrolled scanner-specific texture artifacts that the CNN may exploit as spurious cancer-associated features.
all_issues_adjudicated: The CNN threshold is selected on validation to 'match or exceed' radiologist consensus sensitivity, which guarantees by construction that the CNN cannot appear less sensitive than the radiologist in the primary metric; this engineering of the threshold precludes a fair comparison and introduces threshold selection bias favorable to the CNN.

Architecture selection among ResNet-50 (ImageNet pretrained), custom 3D CNN, and Vision Transformer on the validation set risks selecting the architecture that best fits validation-specific scanner artifacts rather than the one with the best inductive bias for CT-based detection; ImageNet pretraining is a particularly weak starting point for grayscale volumetric medical data.

Cases near the study end date will not have completed the 24-month follow-up required for negative label confirmation; these cases are either excluded (reducing sample size and potentially biasing toward older, more complete records) or included with potentially incorrect negative labels, neither of which is addressed in the design.

Institutional stratification does not guarantee CT acquisition protocol stratification (scanner model, slice thickness, reconstruction kernel); protocol differences that correlate with institution will create uncontrolled scanner-specific texture artifacts that the CNN may exploit as spurious cancer-associated features.
